verses.commentary: Sister affirms her faith. Deacon (diakonos, διάκονος) may mean servant or formal office. Church in Cenchreae (Corinth's eastern port). Phoebe likely carried this letter to Rome. Paul honors her service.

Worthy of the saints means with honor and support. Give help (paristēmi, παρίστημι, stand by, assist) as needed. Benefactor (prostatis, προστάτις, patron, helper) describes Phoebe's role: she supported many, including Paul. She deserves reciprocal care.
rs, Paul's ministry partners (Acts 18). Co-workers (synergos, συνεργός) emphasizes shared labor. In Christ Jesus marks spiritual partnership.
ificial service. When or how is unknown. All Gentile churches are grateful. Their influence was widespread. Faithful service benefits the whole body.
Epenetus: first convert (aparchē, ἀπαρχή, firstfruits) in Asia. Firsts are significant; pioneers hold special honor.
Worked hard (kopiaō, κοπιάω, toil, labor to exhaustion) describes sacrificial service. Many women served prominently in the early church.

Outstanding among the apostles could mean esteemed by apostles or part of the apostolic circle. In Christ before Paul suggests early converts. Length of faith is not superiority, but it is noteworthy.
ul. In the Lord qualifies all Christian relationships. Friendship in Christ is eternal.
Stachys: beloved. These are personal, affectionate greetings. Paul values relationships deeply.
His fidelity (faith) has been tested and found genuine. Household of Aristobulus: possibly slaves or family members. Aristocratic households had many members.
Household of Narcissus: likely a prominent Roman household. In the Lord distinguishes believers in the household; not all were Christians.
Persis: another hard worker (kopiaō, κοπιάω). Beloved (agapētos, ἀγαπητός). Paul honors women's ministry extensively. The early church valued women's contributions.
s). His mother cared for Paul maternally. Christian community creates family bonds beyond biology.
Likely a house church. Brothers and sisters (adelphoi, ἀδελφοί) emphasizes family.
Julia may be Philologus's wife. Nereus and his sister (unnamed). Olympas. All the Lord's people (hagioi, ἅγιοι, saints) encompasses the whole group.
It expressed love and unity. All the churches send greetings through Paul. This connects Roman believers to the universal church.

Watch out (skopeō, σκοπέω, observe, mark) for divisive people. Divisions (dichostasia, διχοστασία, dissensions) and obstacles (skandalon, σκάνδαλον, stumbling blocks) are contrary to apostolic teaching. Keep away (ekklinō, ἐκκλίνω, turn away from) them. False teachers must be avoided.

Smooth talk (chrēstologia, χρηστολογία, fine speech) and flattery (eulogia, εὐλογία, good words) deceive the naive (akakos, ἄκακος, innocent, unsuspecting). False teachers use rhetoric to mislead. Discernment is critical.

Rejoice (chairō, χαίρω). But: be wise (sophos, σοφός) about good and innocent (akeraios, ἀκέραιος, unmixed, pure) about evil. Know good thoroughly; avoid evil completely. Wisdom discerns; innocence protects.

Crush (syntribō, συντρίβω, shatter) echoes Genesis 3:15. Soon (en tachei, ἐν τάχει, speedily) promises imminent victory. Under your feet: believers share in Satan's defeat. Grace (charis, χάρις) benediction. Victory over evil is certain.
Lucius, Jason, Sosipater: fellow Jews (syngenes, συγγενής). These send greetings from Paul's location (likely Corinth). Ministry is collaborative.
He penned Romans as Paul dictated. In the Lord marks him as a believer. This personal touch humanizes the letter. Paul often used secretaries (Romans, 1 Corinthians, Galatians).

Hospitality (xenia, ξενία) is valued. Erastus: city official (oikonomos, οἰκονόμος, treasurer or manager). Christians in government. Quartus: otherwise unknown. The church includes people from all social levels.
Textually disputed (many manuscripts omit it, others include it here or after v. 27). If original, it is a repeated grace benediction. Grace bookends the letter (1:7, 16:24).

To Him shifts focus to God. Able to establish (stērizō, στηρίζω, strengthen, confirm). According to Paul's gospel: the message about Jesus Christ. Revelation of the mystery (mystērion, μυστήριον): once hidden, now revealed. Mystery: Gentile inclusion in God's people, salvation by faith. Hidden for ages (chronois aiōniois, χρόνοις αἰωνίοις, eternal times) but now disclosed.

Through prophetic writings (Old Testament pointed forward). By command of eternal God: divine authority. Purpose: all Gentiles come to obedience of faith (hypakoē pisteōs, ὑπακοὴ πίστεως). Faith produces obedience; the gospel is for all nations. The mystery is now universal.

Glory (doxa, δόξα) belongs to Him alone. Forever (eis tous aiōnas, εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας, unto the ages). Through Jesus Christ: all glory comes through the Son. Amen (so be it) seals the doxology and the letter. Romans ends as it began: with the glory of God in the gospel of Jesus Christ.
